status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Customer support helpdesk with ticket management, knowledge base, live chat, AI auto-response, SLA tracking, and multi-channel inbox (email, social, WhatsApp).

plan.description: Analyze competitor helpdesk platforms (Zendesk, Freshdesk, Intercom, HubSpot Service Hub). Identify pricing tiers, feature gaps, ICP personas (SMB vs enterprise), and WhatsApp/social channel maturity. Produce positioning matrix and differentiation report.
acceptance_criteria: Competitor matrix covers >=5 platforms with pricing, feature, and channel columns
At least 3 ICP personas defined with jobs-to-be-done
Positioning statement differentiating helpdesk_platform from top 2 competitors
WhatsApp Business API integration complexity documented

plan.description: Define business requirements, user stories, and process flows for all six product pillars: ticket management, knowledge base, live chat, AI auto-response, SLA tracking, and multi-channel inbox. Produce BPMN process diagrams for escalation, routing, and SLA breach workflows.
acceptance_criteria: >=40 user stories covering all six pillars, each with acceptance criteria
BPMN diagrams for ticket lifecycle, escalation, and SLA breach
Role-permission matrix covering all 4 user roles
ROI analysis with customer effort score and first-contact resolution KPIs defined
Pricing tiers defined
Churn risk factors identified

plan.description: User research sessions with 8-10 support agents and team leads. Produce wireframes for agent inbox, ticket detail view, live chat panel, knowledge base editor, SLA dashboard, and admin multi-channel setup. Accessibility audit to WCAG 2.1 AA.
acceptance_criteria: 8 research sessions completed with documented findings
Hi-fi wireframes for all 6 core screens in Figma
Component library with 20+ reusable UI components
WCAG 2.1 AA audit report with 0 critical violations
Mobile-responsive layout defined for agent inbox and live chat
App store screenshot mockups prepared
Onboarding flow under 3 steps

plan.description: Produce Product Requirements Document (PRD) covering MoSCoW-prioritized feature list, success metrics (CSAT, FCR, AHT, SLA compliance %), release roadmap with v1/v2/v3 milestones, and API-first strategy for third-party integrations.
acceptance_criteria: PRD approved with MoSCoW triage for all 40+ user stories
Success metrics baseline defined with measurement method for each KPI
3-milestone roadmap with estimated scope and go/no-go criteria per release
API-first integration points documented (webhooks, OAuth2, REST)

plan.description: Build SaaS financial model: per-seat and usage-based pricing tiers (Starter/Growth/Enterprise), CAC/LTV projections, infrastructure cost model (compute, storage, WhatsApp API per-message fees), 36-month P&L, and break-even analysis.
acceptance_criteria: 36-month P&L with 3 growth scenarios (base, bull, bear)
Pricing tiers validated against competitor benchmarks from step 1
Infrastructure cost per customer modeled at 3 scale points (100, 1k, 10k tickets/day)
Break-even month identified per scenario
MRR/ARR projections modeled
CAC and LTV estimated

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy (GDPR/CCPA compliant), Data Processing Agreement (DPA) for enterprise customers, and WhatsApp Business API acceptable-use compliance review. Define data retention and deletion SLAs.
acceptance_criteria: ToS and Privacy Policy reviewed against GDPR Art. 13/14 disclosure requirements
DPA template includes SCCs for EU data transfers
WhatsApp AUP review confirms acceptable use for customer support workflows
Data deletion endpoint and 30-day SLA documented in Privacy Policy
Subscription terms cover cancellation and refunds
Data processing agreement drafted

plan.description: Design multi-tenant PostgreSQL schema covering: tenants, users/roles, tickets, ticket_events (audit log), messages, channels, knowledge_articles, article_feedback, sla_policies, sla_breaches, tags, canned_responses, and ai_suggestion_log. Include Alembic migration scripts and seed data.
acceptance_criteria: ERD reviewed and approved, all FK constraints defined
Alembic migration baseline runs clean on fresh PostgreSQL instance
Full-text search index on knowledge_articles tested with 10k rows
Row-level security policies implemented for tenant isolation
Seed script populates demo tenant with 50 tickets, 20 KB articles

plan.description: Define OpenAPI 3.1 spec for all REST endpoints: auth (OAuth2/JWT), tickets CRUD, messages, knowledge base CRUD, live chat WebSocket protocol, SLA policies, channel configuration, webhook subscriptions, AI suggestion endpoints, and analytics. Version all endpoints under /v1/.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec passes swagger-parser validation with 0 errors
All endpoints documented with request/response schemas and error codes
WebSocket protocol message types documented (connect, message, typing, close)
Rate limiting headers documented on all endpoints

plan.description: Set up project configuration: monorepo structure (backend/frontend/infra), environment variable schema (.env.example), Docker Compose for local dev (PostgreSQL, Redis, Minio, MailHog), pre-commit hooks, ESLint/Black/Ruff linting, and coordinator agentic pattern config for AI auto-response pipeline.

Pre-commit hooks block commits with lint errors

plan.description: Implement core ticket management service: create/read/update/close tickets, assign to agents/teams, priority/status state machine (new->open->pending->on_hold->resolved->closed), tagging, internal notes, file attachments (S3/Minio), ticket merging, bulk operations, and full audit event log per ticket.

plan.description: Implement knowledge base service: article CRUD with versioning, category/subcategory tree, full-text search (PostgreSQL tsvector), article publish/draft/archive workflow, SEO slug generation, article feedback (thumbs up/down), and public-facing search endpoint for embeddable widget.

payload.versioning: article_versions table, diff available on request

acceptance_criteria: Full-text search returns ranked results in under 100ms for 10k articles
Article versioning stores diff and author per version

plan.description: Implement SLA tracking service: configurable SLA policies (first response time, resolution time by priority/channel/business-hours), SLA clock start/pause/resume logic, breach prediction warn at 75%, breach events, compliance reporting by team/agent/period, and holiday calendar support.

plan.description: Implement AI auto-response system using coordinator pattern. IntakeClassifierAgent tags intent/sentiment/language. KBSearchAgent retrieves top-3 relevant articles via pgvector semantic search. AutoResponseAgent drafts reply using LLM plus KB context plus past resolutions. EscalationAgent decides auto-send vs agent-review based on confidence threshold. Coordinator orchestrates per incoming ticket or message.

agents.intake_classifier: classifies intent, sentiment, language, urgency — tools: ticket_reader, tag_writer
agents.kb_search: semantic search over knowledge_articles embeddings — tools: vector_search, article_fetcher
agents.auto_response: drafts reply from LLM plus context — tools: llm_generate, template_engine
agents.escalation: routes to agent if confidence below threshold — tools: ticket_assign, notification_send

payload.vector_store: pgvector extension on knowledge_articles embeddings column
payload.audit: all suggestions logged to ai_suggestion_log with trace_id, confidence, and cited articles

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator pipeline completes intake to kb to response to escalation in under 3 seconds p95
Auto-response sent without agent review only when confidence is >= 0.82
All AI suggestions logged with confidence score and KB article IDs cited
Escalation fires for tickets with confidence below threshold, assigns to queue
Language detection routes non-English tickets to language-specific agent queues
A/B test harness in place to compare AI response vs human response CSAT over time

Generate KiCad files, BOM with supplier part numbers, and Gerber files passing DFM.

acceptance_criteria: Unity test suite builds and runs in QEMU CI with 0 failures
>=75% branch coverage on OTA update state machine via gcov

Secure boot HIL test: corrupted firmware image rejected and original boots successfully

flaws: Step 7 contradicts itself: specifies 'schema_per_tenant' multi-tenancy AND row-level security policies. These are mutually exclusive strategies. Schema-per-tenant isolates at the schema boundary; RLS operates within a shared schema. Choosing both wastes engineering time and creates ambiguous security guarantees.

Step 21 fundamentally misunderstands SOC 2 Type II. Type II requires a minimum 6-month auditor observation period of controls in operation. You cannot 'produce artifacts' and claim SOC 2 Type II. This plan will cause enterprise sales failures when customers request audit reports. Step 21 should target SOC 2 Type I initially.

Step 18 acceptance criteria of 'under 3KB gzipped' for a vanilla JS widget with WebSocket, file upload, proactive triggers, pre-chat form, CSAT survey, and offline fallback is not achievable. A minimal WebSocket client alone exceeds this. Intercom, Drift, and Crisp ship 40-100KB widgets. This criteria will be quietly dropped or the feature set gutted.

Steps 30-33 (firmware, QEMU simulation, PCB design, HIL testing) are a completely separate hardware product embedded in a SaaS helpdesk build plan. PCB design for an optional appliance has no business being in the critical path. This adds 4 sequential steps of hardware engineering risk to a software product launch.

WhatsApp Business API constraints are critically underestimated in Step 14. After the 24-hour customer service window closes, outbound messages MUST use pre-approved Message Templates. Free-form replies are not allowed for business-initiated messages. Auto-responses (Step 15) sent outside this window will be rejected by the API, breaking a core product promise.

Step 15 sets a confidence threshold of 0.82 with no calibration basis. Auto-sending LLM-generated responses to customers without human review at an arbitrary threshold is a customer satisfaction risk. The A/B test harness is listed as an acceptance criteria for the same step that auto-sends — you cannot A/B test a threshold you haven't validated yet.

Step 12 inconsistently specifies 'Redis pub/sub' in the description and 'Redis Streams' in the payload. These have different semantics: pub/sub is fire-and-forget; Streams are persistent. The offline message queue requirement in the same step requires persistence, so pub/sub alone is incorrect. The implementation team will make an arbitrary choice.

Step 20 (security threat modeling) depends on Step 15 (AI system built). STRIDE threat modeling must happen before implementation, not after. By Step 20, all six pillars are already coded. Architectural security findings at this stage require rework, not mitigations.

Step 17 introduces 'Next.js or Vite SSR' as an unresolved option for public KB pages. Step 9 already established a Vite monorepo. Introducing Next.js in Step 17 is a major architectural decision that affects the build system, hosting, routing, and SSR hydration strategy. This decision must be made in Step 9, not deferred.

Step 32 (PCB design) lists Step 31 (QEMU simulation) as its dependency. This is inverted. You need hardware specifications to design a simulation — the simulation is derived from the hardware design, not a prerequisite for it.

No billing or subscription management step exists. The financial model (Step 5) defines per-seat and usage-based pricing tiers, but there is no implementation step for Stripe (or equivalent), subscription lifecycle management, usage metering for message-based billing, or dunning. Without billing, this is not a shippable product.
suggestions: Resolve the multi-tenancy strategy in Step 7 before writing a line of code. Choose either schema-per-tenant (stronger isolation, harder migrations, higher PostgreSQL connection overhead) or shared-schema with RLS (simpler ops, requires rigorous policy testing). Document the decision in an ADR.

Replace Step 21's SOC 2 Type II target with SOC 2 Type I for launch. Begin the 6-month Type II observation period immediately post-launch. Update the legal docs and sales collateral to accurately reflect which report is available.

Insert a billing implementation step between Steps 9 and 10. Stripe integration, webhook-driven subscription state machine, and usage metering (for message-based tiers) are blocking for any paid customer. This is more foundational than the firmware steps.

Move security threat modeling (Step 20) to before Step 7 (database design). Run STRIDE on the architecture document, not the finished code. Findings should inform schema design, API contracts, and agent boundaries — not retrofit controls post-build.

Define the pgvector strategy in Step 7. The database schema step must include the vector embeddings column on knowledge_articles, the pgvector extension, and the embedding generation trigger (on article create/update). Step 15 currently assumes this infrastructure exists but Step 7 does not create it.

Cut the 3KB widget constraint in Step 18 to a realistic 25-30KB gzipped budget. Document the actual constraint rationale. If load time is the concern, address it with async loading and lazy initialization, not an impossible size limit.

Add a WhatsApp Message Template management UI and workflow to Steps 14 and 19. The AI auto-response system (Step 15) must be aware of the 24-hour session window and switch to approved templates for out-of-window messages. This is not optional — it is a Meta platform requirement.

Make Steps 30-33 a separate, explicitly optional hardware product workstream. Remove them from the main dependency chain. The core SaaS product should reach production without any dependency on PCB Gerbers.

Add DKIM/SPF/DMARC configuration and email deliverability monitoring to Step 14. Outbound email replies will land in spam without proper DNS records. Postmaster tools for Gmail and Outlook should be set up as part of channel configuration.

Add a data migration tooling step for enterprise onboarding. Zendesk and Freshdesk both offer data export APIs. Without a migration path, enterprise deals will stall at procurement because customers cannot justify switching cost without data portability.

Twitter/X DM API access now requires paid tiers and is subject to frequent policy changes. Add an explicit risk flag and cost line item. Consider making X/Twitter a v2 feature and removing it from the core launch scope to reduce third-party API dependency risk.
missing: Billing and subscription management implementation (Stripe or equivalent) — required to charge customers
pgvector extension and embedding generation pipeline in the database schema step
WhatsApp Message Template management and 24-hour session window handling
Email deliverability infrastructure: DKIM, SPF, DMARC, bounce handling, unsubscribe list management
Customer data migration tooling from Zendesk and Freshdesk
Feature flag system for progressive rollout and safe production deployments
Multi-region data residency strategy for GDPR compliance (EU data must stay in EU)
API rate limiting implementation details — only referenced, never specified (Redis sliding window? Token bucket? Which library?)
Webhook replay attack prevention beyond idempotency key (timestamp validation, replay window)
Agent performance reporting and CSAT attribution to individual agents (required for team lead personas defined in Step 2)
Async file upload virus scan race condition mitigation — ClamAV scan is async but file URL is returned before scan completes
SOC 2 Type I vs Type II clarification and realistic timeline for achieving each
security_risks: Schema-per-tenant ambiguity: if the application uses a PostgreSQL superuser connection, a SQL injection in one tenant's schema can affect all other tenants' schemas. The connection user must be scoped per schema with revoked cross-schema privileges.

AI prompt injection via ticket content is listed as a concern but the mitigation ('input sanitization') is insufficient for LLM prompt injection. Ticket content will be embedded directly in LLM prompts (Step 15). Sanitization that works for XSS does not prevent prompt injection. Requires structural prompt design with clear input/instruction separation.

ClamAV async scan window: Step 10 returns a signed S3 URL within 2 seconds, but the virus scan is listed as an async Celery task. This means a malicious file is accessible via signed URL before it is scanned. The signed URL must not be returned until the scan completes or the URL must be invalidated post-scan-failure.

WhatsApp webhook spoofing: Step 20 lists HMAC verification as a control, but Step 14 implements the webhook handlers. There is no acceptance criteria in Step 14 requiring HMAC verification. This gap means the webhook could ship without verification and only be caught in Step 20.

JWT RS256 private key management is unspecified. Key storage (HSM? KMS? Secret Manager?), rotation schedule, and emergency revocation procedure are absent. A compromised signing key invalidates all user sessions and cannot be rotated without disruption.

The embeddable KB widget (Step 11, Step 17) is unauthenticated and rate-limited to 60 req/min. A distributed scraper hitting this endpoint from multiple IPs can exfiltrate the entire knowledge base. Content sensitivity classification and robots.txt/noindex controls are not addressed.

Chat widget loaded via script tag (Step 18) on third-party sites is a supply chain injection risk. If the CDN serving the widget JS is compromised, all customers' websites are compromised. Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash enforcement is not mentioned.
final_review.summary: This plan is architecturally thorough for a B2B SaaS helpdesk but has several production-blocking gaps that prevent a score above 65. The most critical failures are: (1) no billing implementation step — the product cannot charge customers without it; (2) SOC 2 Type II is listed as a deliverable but requires a 6-month auditor observation period that cannot be produced on a build timeline; (3) the schema-per-tenant and RLS contradiction will cause a security architecture debate mid-build; (4) the WhatsApp 24-hour session window and Message Template requirement are not accounted for in the AI auto-response design, meaning a core feature will be blocked by Meta's API policies; and (5) Steps 30-33 embed an entirely separate hardware product (PCB design, firmware, QEMU simulation) into the critical path of a SaaS launch. The core software plan — ticket management, live chat, knowledge base, SLA tracking, multi-channel — is well-specified with concrete acceptance criteria and a logical dependency chain. The security and compliance steps exist but are sequenced too late. The 3KB widget constraint and the arbitrary 0.82 confidence threshold for autonomous AI replies are acceptance criteria that will either be silently dropped or cause customer-facing failures. Recommend: cut hardware to a separate initiative, add billing as Step 9.5, move threat modeling before Step 7, resolve the multi-tenancy strategy before database design, and replace SOC 2 Type II with Type I for launch.

flaws: Step 7 contradicts itself: specifies 'schema_per_tenant' multi-tenancy AND row-level security policies. These are mutually exclusive strategies. Schema-per-tenant isolates at the schema boundary; RLS operates within a shared schema. Choosing both wastes engineering time and creates ambiguous security guarantees.

Step 21 fundamentally misunderstands SOC 2 Type II. Type II requires a minimum 6-month auditor observation period of controls in operation. You cannot 'produce artifacts' and claim SOC 2 Type II. This plan will cause enterprise sales failures when customers request audit reports. Step 21 should target SOC 2 Type I initially.

Step 18 acceptance criteria of 'under 3KB gzipped' for a vanilla JS widget with WebSocket, file upload, proactive triggers, pre-chat form, CSAT survey, and offline fallback is not achievable. A minimal WebSocket client alone exceeds this. Intercom, Drift, and Crisp ship 40-100KB widgets. This criteria will be quietly dropped or the feature set gutted.

Steps 30-33 (firmware, QEMU simulation, PCB design, HIL testing) are a completely separate hardware product embedded in a SaaS helpdesk build plan. PCB design for an optional appliance has no business being in the critical path. This adds 4 sequential steps of hardware engineering risk to a software product launch.

WhatsApp Business API constraints are critically underestimated in Step 14. After the 24-hour customer service window closes, outbound messages MUST use pre-approved Message Templates. Free-form replies are not allowed for business-initiated messages. Auto-responses (Step 15) sent outside this window will be rejected by the API, breaking a core product promise.

Step 15 sets a confidence threshold of 0.82 with no calibration basis. Auto-sending LLM-generated responses to customers without human review at an arbitrary threshold is a customer satisfaction risk. The A/B test harness is listed as an acceptance criteria for the same step that auto-sends — you cannot A/B test a threshold you haven't validated yet.

Step 12 inconsistently specifies 'Redis pub/sub' in the description and 'Redis Streams' in the payload. These have different semantics: pub/sub is fire-and-forget; Streams are persistent. The offline message queue requirement in the same step requires persistence, so pub/sub alone is incorrect. The implementation team will make an arbitrary choice.

Step 20 (security threat modeling) depends on Step 15 (AI system built). STRIDE threat modeling must happen before implementation, not after. By Step 20, all six pillars are already coded. Architectural security findings at this stage require rework, not mitigations.

Step 17 introduces 'Next.js or Vite SSR' as an unresolved option for public KB pages. Step 9 already established a Vite monorepo. Introducing Next.js in Step 17 is a major architectural decision that affects the build system, hosting, routing, and SSR hydration strategy. This decision must be made in Step 9, not deferred.

Step 32 (PCB design) lists Step 31 (QEMU simulation) as its dependency. This is inverted. You need hardware specifications to design a simulation — the simulation is derived from the hardware design, not a prerequisite for it.

No billing or subscription management step exists. The financial model (Step 5) defines per-seat and usage-based pricing tiers, but there is no implementation step for Stripe (or equivalent), subscription lifecycle management, usage metering for message-based billing, or dunning. Without billing, this is not a shippable product.
suggestions: Resolve the multi-tenancy strategy in Step 7 before writing a line of code. Choose either schema-per-tenant (stronger isolation, harder migrations, higher PostgreSQL connection overhead) or shared-schema with RLS (simpler ops, requires rigorous policy testing). Document the decision in an ADR.

Replace Step 21's SOC 2 Type II target with SOC 2 Type I for launch. Begin the 6-month Type II observation period immediately post-launch. Update the legal docs and sales collateral to accurately reflect which report is available.

Insert a billing implementation step between Steps 9 and 10. Stripe integration, webhook-driven subscription state machine, and usage metering (for message-based tiers) are blocking for any paid customer. This is more foundational than the firmware steps.

Move security threat modeling (Step 20) to before Step 7 (database design). Run STRIDE on the architecture document, not the finished code. Findings should inform schema design, API contracts, and agent boundaries — not retrofit controls post-build.

Define the pgvector strategy in Step 7. The database schema step must include the vector embeddings column on knowledge_articles, the pgvector extension, and the embedding generation trigger (on article create/update). Step 15 currently assumes this infrastructure exists but Step 7 does not create it.

Cut the 3KB widget constraint in Step 18 to a realistic 25-30KB gzipped budget. Document the actual constraint rationale. If load time is the concern, address it with async loading and lazy initialization, not an impossible size limit.

Add a WhatsApp Message Template management UI and workflow to Steps 14 and 19. The AI auto-response system (Step 15) must be aware of the 24-hour session window and switch to approved templates for out-of-window messages. This is not optional — it is a Meta platform requirement.

Make Steps 30-33 a separate, explicitly optional hardware product workstream. Remove them from the main dependency chain. The core SaaS product should reach production without any dependency on PCB Gerbers.

Add DKIM/SPF/DMARC configuration and email deliverability monitoring to Step 14. Outbound email replies will land in spam without proper DNS records. Postmaster tools for Gmail and Outlook should be set up as part of channel configuration.

Add a data migration tooling step for enterprise onboarding. Zendesk and Freshdesk both offer data export APIs. Without a migration path, enterprise deals will stall at procurement because customers cannot justify switching cost without data portability.

Twitter/X DM API access now requires paid tiers and is subject to frequent policy changes. Add an explicit risk flag and cost line item. Consider making X/Twitter a v2 feature and removing it from the core launch scope to reduce third-party API dependency risk.
missing: Billing and subscription management implementation (Stripe or equivalent) — required to charge customers
pgvector extension and embedding generation pipeline in the database schema step
WhatsApp Message Template management and 24-hour session window handling
Email deliverability infrastructure: DKIM, SPF, DMARC, bounce handling, unsubscribe list management
Customer data migration tooling from Zendesk and Freshdesk
Feature flag system for progressive rollout and safe production deployments
Multi-region data residency strategy for GDPR compliance (EU data must stay in EU)
API rate limiting implementation details — only referenced, never specified (Redis sliding window? Token bucket? Which library?)
Webhook replay attack prevention beyond idempotency key (timestamp validation, replay window)
Agent performance reporting and CSAT attribution to individual agents (required for team lead personas defined in Step 2)
Async file upload virus scan race condition mitigation — ClamAV scan is async but file URL is returned before scan completes
SOC 2 Type I vs Type II clarification and realistic timeline for achieving each
security_risks: Schema-per-tenant ambiguity: if the application uses a PostgreSQL superuser connection, a SQL injection in one tenant's schema can affect all other tenants' schemas. The connection user must be scoped per schema with revoked cross-schema privileges.

AI prompt injection via ticket content is listed as a concern but the mitigation ('input sanitization') is insufficient for LLM prompt injection. Ticket content will be embedded directly in LLM prompts (Step 15). Sanitization that works for XSS does not prevent prompt injection. Requires structural prompt design with clear input/instruction separation.

ClamAV async scan window: Step 10 returns a signed S3 URL within 2 seconds, but the virus scan is listed as an async Celery task. This means a malicious file is accessible via signed URL before it is scanned. The signed URL must not be returned until the scan completes or the URL must be invalidated post-scan-failure.

WhatsApp webhook spoofing: Step 20 lists HMAC verification as a control, but Step 14 implements the webhook handlers. There is no acceptance criteria in Step 14 requiring HMAC verification. This gap means the webhook could ship without verification and only be caught in Step 20.

JWT RS256 private key management is unspecified. Key storage (HSM? KMS? Secret Manager?), rotation schedule, and emergency revocation procedure are absent. A compromised signing key invalidates all user sessions and cannot be rotated without disruption.

The embeddable KB widget (Step 11, Step 17) is unauthenticated and rate-limited to 60 req/min. A distributed scraper hitting this endpoint from multiple IPs can exfiltrate the entire knowledge base. Content sensitivity classification and robots.txt/noindex controls are not addressed.

Chat widget loaded via script tag (Step 18) on third-party sites is a supply chain injection risk. If the CDN serving the widget JS is compromised, all customers' websites are compromised. Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash enforcement is not mentioned.
history.summary: This plan is architecturally thorough for a B2B SaaS helpdesk but has several production-blocking gaps that prevent a score above 65. The most critical failures are: (1) no billing implementation step — the product cannot charge customers without it; (2) SOC 2 Type II is listed as a deliverable but requires a 6-month auditor observation period that cannot be produced on a build timeline; (3) the schema-per-tenant and RLS contradiction will cause a security architecture debate mid-build; (4) the WhatsApp 24-hour session window and Message Template requirement are not accounted for in the AI auto-response design, meaning a core feature will be blocked by Meta's API policies; and (5) Steps 30-33 embed an entirely separate hardware product (PCB design, firmware, QEMU simulation) into the critical path of a SaaS launch. The core software plan — ticket management, live chat, knowledge base, SLA tracking, multi-channel — is well-specified with concrete acceptance criteria and a logical dependency chain. The security and compliance steps exist but are sequenced too late. The 3KB widget constraint and the arbitrary 0.82 confidence threshold for autonomous AI replies are acceptance criteria that will either be silently dropped or cause customer-facing failures. Recommend: cut hardware to a separate initiative, add billing as Step 9.5, move threat modeling before Step 7, resolve the multi-tenancy strategy before database design, and replace SOC 2 Type II with Type I for launch.
